# GeniusGTX Writing Style Guide

**Status:** Source of truth for all GeniusGTX long-form content. Cloud routines fetch this file at runtime via raw GitHub. Edit here, then push — do not edit the cloud routine prompt directly.

**Companion docs:**
- [hook-writing.md](./hook-writing.md) — opener types, compositional spine, swipe-file reference
- [cta-template.md](./cta-template.md) — the 5-part close with reciprocity bridge
- [community-registry.md](./community-registry.md) — X community routing rules
- [swipe-file.md](./swipe-file.md) — proven viral hooks for inspiration

---

## The Big Picture

GeniusGTX is a gallery for the greatest minds in economics, psychology, and history. The voice carries weight without announcing itself. The reader finishes a piece and feels smarter, not lectured to.

Every piece moves through the same emotional sequence: **fascination first, then the uncomfortable truth, then a conclusion that leaves the reader with something they can carry.**

---

## ONE BIG IDEA per post (non-negotiable)

**Each post is the complete development of a single insight.** Everything in the body serves its proof, deepening, or application.

- No tours ("3 lessons from Marie Curie")
- No listicles
- No "and another thing"
- The story is the proof of one universal law, not a survey of facts

The Semmelweis story is not a story about Semmelweis. It is the proof of one insight about how institutions defend the problems that justify them.

If you find yourself adding a second insight, it is probably its own post. Save it.

---

## Length

| | Body | Total post (incl. CTA) |
|---|---|---|
| Target | 1100–1300 chars (~200 words) | 1300–1450 chars |
| Floor | 160 words body = thin | — |
| Ceiling | 230 words body = trim | 1500 hard |

**Format:** single continuous long-form post on X. **No follow-up reply tweet.** The 5-part CTA is inline at the end (see [cta-template.md](./cta-template.md)).

---

## Voice

- **Systems thinker.** Diagnose mechanisms. Never moralize.
- **Incentive-first.** Identify who benefits before accepting any narrative.
- **Calibrated urgency.** Never doom. Never hype.
- **Reader is smart, slightly frustrated, beginning to see the rules weren't written for them.**
- **Exit feeling:** the world is more legible, and the reader has one actionable thing.

### Founder voice (Toản Truong)

The displacement between people who own AI systems and people who work for them is happening now, not someday. The system isn't broken — it's working exactly as designed. The middle class made a deal: stability for capped upside. That deal is being renegotiated without their consent.

Skill without ownership is well-paid employment. Most people learn this too late. Follow the money and the incentives — in geopolitics, institutions, business, and advice. Before you believe anything, find who benefits most from you believing it.

---

## Truth Over Trust

The goal is never to convince. It is to **show the reader what has to be true.**

- **Never start with the claim and back it up.** Start with the obstacle, then build causally to the claim. The claim is the conclusion the reader reaches, not the premise they are asked to accept.
- **Use IF/THEN structure** instead of assertion. "If this measurement has been wrong for 20 years, then every policy built on it carries the same error." Let the logic declare the significance.
- **Sequence facts causally.** Each fact must require the next. Test: can you swap any two facts without losing meaning? If yes, reorder until the answer is no.
- **One mechanism per post.** Find the single most load-bearing causal link. Explain it clearly. Everything else stays lean.
- **No assertion lines.** Cut any line that says "this changes everything," "nobody is talking about this," or "this is huge." Replace each with the structural reason it matters.

---

## Body Structure

```
Hook (opener line, under 20 words — see hook-writing.md)
Wall (line 2: why this couldn't have happened before — the structural condition)
Build (the story, escalating beat by beat)
Suppression / Incentive Layer (1 sentence: who benefits from the reader not knowing)
I-Factor (OPTIONAL, 1–2 sentences: author as pattern tracker)
Contrast Close → Aphoristic Punch (the landing)
[5-part CTA — see cta-template.md]
```

### Hook → Wall transition (Truth-Structure Transition)

The hook grabs attention. **Line 2 begins building truth — not amplifying the hook.**

- Hook = what happened (signal, pattern interrupt, scroll-stop)
- Line 2 = why it couldn't have happened before (the wall)

The wall does not need to be long. It needs to be the right obstacle — the one that makes every fact that follows feel earned rather than asserted.

### Suppression / Incentive Layer

After the build, one sentence naming the entity or structure that benefits from the reader not knowing this. **Not conspiracy language — structural analysis.**

Example: *"The institution that profited from her discoveries worked quietly to keep her out."* — that's the suppression layer. Never says "they hid the truth." Says: here is the structural beneficiary of the lie.

### I-Factor (optional)

1–2 sentences positioning the author as someone who tracks these patterns. **Credibility on the method, not opinion on the topic.**

- *"I've been tracking this pattern across dozens of economic systems."*
- *"I've spent the last three years studying how institutions defend their founding errors."*

**Use sparingly.** Once every 1–2 weeks max. If forced, it reads as performative. Skip it when the story carries the weight.

### Contrast Close → Aphoristic Punch

The default landing is a **two-step move**:

1. **Contrast Close** — name the assumption, then name the reality. Two sentences.
   *"The income didn't shrink. The cost of standing still did."*
2. **Aphoristic Punch** — one compressed sentence that states the principle as if it were a discovered law.
   *"Every institution eventually defends the problem that justifies its prestige."*

The contrast builds the tension. The aphorism crystallizes. Reader leaves carrying one sentence.

**Optional third move — name the mental model.** When the post is teaching a transferable lens, name it explicitly right before the CTA bridge. *"That's the inversion model."* / *"The first-order story is X. The second-order story is Y."* Giving the insight a portable label makes the toolkit CTA land on concrete ground — the reader just experienced the framework the toolkit maps.

---

## Narrative Patterns (observed from top-performing posts)

These are principles, not a fixed structure. A post can hit one, several, or all of them depending on the story. They are what separates a prose narrative from an info dump.

### Name the opponent

Never "critics pushed back" or "the industry dismissed it." Name the person, their title, their institution, the exact action.

- ✗ *"Egyptologists dismissed the theory."*
- ✓ *"UCL Egyptologist David Jeffreys dismissed Houdin's theory as 'far-fetched and horribly complicated.'"*

- ✗ *"The board disagreed with him."*
- ✓ *"Ilya Sutskever, OpenAI's co-founder, concluded Altman was 'not someone who should press the AGI button.' He tried to fire him. Microsoft brought Altman back within days."*

A named opponent gives the reader a face to hold the tension against. Generic dismissal is forgettable. Specific dismissal is a scene.

### Prose first, → bullets as the exception

Default to prose paragraphs. Use `→` bullets only when:

1. The content is genuinely a list (e.g., Dimon's own 5 interview questions — quoted from him).
2. A compressed payoff-stack of 3–5 tight facts lands harder as visual rhythm than as prose.
3. The body is Tuki QRT format (bullets are common there).

**Test:** if the content could be a paragraph without losing anything, make it a paragraph. `→` stacks are memorable when rare — when every post is a bullet list, none of them are.

### The mid-body devastator

Somewhere in the middle of the body, drop **one short line** that breaks the reader's assumption and re-angles the story. It's usually 3–8 words, often a fragment.

- *"None of them."* (after three parallel Altman claims)
- *"The mechanism had nothing to do with comfort."* (after the sleep-position stat)
- *"Here's what nobody says out loud."* (before the HSBC systems reveal)
- *"And suddenly everything fit."* (after the pyramid ramp math failed)

The devastator is the pivot where "interesting facts" becomes "holy shit, the premise was wrong." Every strong post has one. Write it before you write the final aphorism.

### Timeline as pacing device

Specific years inside the body move the story forward, not just anchor it in the hook. Each year advances the case.

- Pyramid: *1986 microgravimetric survey → 1999 Houdin ran the math → 2017 muon radiography detected the void.*
- Sleep: *"In 2004, researchers discovered…"* lands mid-body after the setup.

When a story has multiple dated events, **sequence them as a timeline** rather than dropping them in passing. The march of years is its own proof.

### Parallel setup + line-reveal (hook option)

A proven hook move — not a required one:

> *Sam Altman told Congress AGI would "cure cancer, solve climate change, and eliminate poverty."*
> *He told Microsoft it would "generate hundreds of billions in revenue."*
> *He told consumers it was "the best digital assistant."*
> *Karen Hao interviewed 90 current and former OpenAI employees to find out which version was real.*
> *None of them.*

Three parallel claims → one-line devastator. Use when the story is fundamentally about contradiction, misdirection, or betrayal. Don't force it on stories that don't have the three-audiences-one-actor shape.

### Named protagonist + credentials

Not "a researcher" but "**a French architect named Jean-Pierre Houdin**." Not "the CEO" but "**Georges Elhedery. He runs HSBC — $3 trillion in assets, the world's 4th largest bank.**"

The title + institution combo does scroll-stop work on its own. If a protagonist is unnamed in the draft, pull their credential from the source and name them.

### Aphorisms stack — specific then universal

The strongest closes land twice: the specific insight first, then zoom out to the universal principle.

**HSBC:**
> *The first-order story is AI cutting jobs. The second-order story is AI revealing which jobs were always phantom overhead.*
> *Understanding that difference is what separates clear analysis from headlines.*

**Sleep:**
> *Most of what shapes your mind happens before you're awake enough to notice.*
> *Your physiology broadcasts a state that precedes and shapes your psychology.*

**Altman:**
> *The destination was never AGI. It was the journey.*
> *Because a destination means arrival. And arrival means accountability.*

Specific aphorism = the lesson the reader just earned. Universal aphorism = the portable mental model. Reader leaves with both. Still counts as one "aphoristic punch" against the 2-per-post cap (the two layers together are the punch).

---

## Aphorisms — woven through, not just at the close

**Maximum 2 per post.** Scarcity is what makes them hit.

**Four valid positions:**
1. **Hook pivot** — replace a generic pivot phrase with a compressed line. *"Here's what happens when a system discovers its own blind spot:"*
2. **Between beats** — punctuate a load-bearing build moment. *"Power defends the problem that justifies it."*
3. **The turn** — the moment the specific story becomes a universal law.
4. **The close** — the Aphoristic Punch.

**Rules that keep aphorisms from overcooking:**
- Present tense. No hedge. *"Every institution eventually defends..."* not *"Most institutions tend to..."*
- No author voice inside them. *No "I think,"* no *"we see that,"* no *"in my experience."* They read as discovered laws, not opinions.
- Earned by the specific mechanism just explained. A generic aphorism that could live on any post is a failure.
- Iceberg rule holds. Simple on the surface, weight underneath. If it sounds clever, cut it. If it sounds obvious but reframes the whole post, keep it.

---

## Sentence Rules

- **Hard cap: 18 words.** Sentences exceeding 18 words must split. No exceptions.
- **Natural pace within the cap.** Not artificially short and punchy — that reads like a content template. Use the full 18 words when the idea earns them.
- **One idea per sentence.**
- **Vary length deliberately.** A 6-word sentence followed by a 15-word sentence followed by a 4-word sentence is the engine. Three same-length sentences in a row is the enemy.
- **No em dashes.** Split into new sentences instead. Almost every em dash is a place where two sentences want to exist but got forced together.

### Sentence variety requirement

Every post contains at least **4 distinct sentence shapes** across these:
- Fragment (1–3 words): *"Same hospital." / "And then they fired him."*
- Question: *"The only real difference?"*
- Imperative: *"Wash your hands between autopsies and deliveries."*
- Short declarative (4–8 words)
- Long load-bearing (12–18 words)
- Aphoristic: *"Every institution eventually defends the problem that justifies its prestige."*

Three declaratives in a row is the enemy — not because they're long, but because they're the same shape.

---

## Reading Level — Target 6th Grade (Flesch-Kincaid 6.0–7.5)

This is where Hemingway, Churchill, and most high-performing social content live.

**Substitutions (default):**
- "establishment" → "top doctors"
- "forcing him out of the hospital" → "pushed him out"
- "committing him to an asylum" → "locked him in a mental asylum"
- "confirming his theory" → "proving him right"
- "formally adopted" → "finally accepted"
- "the cost compounds" → "the cost gets bigger"

**Keep technical when accuracy demands.** *Pathologist, autopsies, chlorinate, perceptron* — these earn their syllables because they're specific. Specificity beats simplicity when a generic word would lose meaning.

---

## Filler — Warmth vs. Padding

There is no banned-words list for filler. Use the **padding test**:

> Before every filler word: can you cut it with no tonal loss? If yes, cut. If no (it's doing real work), keep.

**Padding (cut):**
- *"this is basically a mistake"* → *"this is a mistake"* (basically adds nothing)
- *"really significant"* → *"significant"*
- *"actually happens"* → *"happens"*

**Warmth (keep):**
- *"Virchow had basically helped kill a generation"* — "basically" softens a massive accusation; that's voice
- *"finally accepted"* — carries decades of holdout
- *"figured out something pretty simple"* — opener softens the entry

Words that often pad: *really, just, basically, essentially, somewhat, rather, actually, simply, truly*. Words that often warm: *pretty, kind of, honestly, finally, whole, entire, real*. Same words can do either job depending on context. Trust the test.

---

## Banned AI Words

Never use:
> delve, tapestry, nuanced, pivotal, groundbreaking, transformative, foster, leverage, utilize, paradigm, holistic, synergy, robust, comprehensive, multifaceted, underscores, highlights

These signal AI-generated prose to the reader. Cut on sight.

## Banned Openers

Never start with:
> "In today's world", "It's no secret", "Throughout history"

## Banned Transitions

Never use:
> Furthermore, Moreover, Additionally, In conclusion

## Banned Hedging

Never write:
> "might potentially", "could possibly", "seems to suggest"

## Banned Twitter Patterns

Never write:
> "Let that sink in", "Nobody is talking about it", "Here's the thing"

---

## Direct Quotes — the Reporting Voice

**Minimum 1, target 2 attributed direct quotes per post.**

- Pull direct quotes in quotation marks. Always attributed by name.
- At least 1 from the **primary source** (the person at the center of the story).
- Optional 2nd from an **opposing authority, critic, or contemporaneous observer** for contrast.
- One quote per section. Two back-to-back = transcript summary.
- After the quote, **don't explain it.** Trust the reader. Move forward.

When scanning a transcript or source for usable quotes, look for three types:
1. **Counterintuitive moments** — the speaker contradicts what most people assume. Highest-value.
2. **Concrete moments** — abstract becomes specific. A number, a date, a decision.
3. **Vulnerable moments** — the speaker admits uncertainty. Builds trust.

Lead with your own sentence first. The quote reinforces an idea you've already stated. It never opens cold and never carries the idea on its own.

---

## Formatting

- Blank line above and below every paragraph.
- Max 2 sentences per paragraph (occasional 3 when rhythm demands).
- One idea per paragraph.
- **Load-bearing line rule:** any sentence carrying a contrarian claim, aphorism, pivot, or reframing specific gets its own paragraph with an extra blank line below.
- **Scan test before shipping:** thumb-scroll the post on your phone. If any block is 3+ lines without a break, split it.

### "→" arrow facts (optional in long-form, default in Tuki QRT)

Use "→" to mark factual beats that should escalate by emotional weight.

```
→ Each fact gets its own line.
→ Blank line between each →.
→ Order by weight, not just causal sequence — last fact is the one the reader cannot forget.
→ Setup/payoff pairing — facts work in pairs. First establishes expectation. Second breaks or confirms it.
→ Never two setups back-to-back. Never two payoffs back-to-back.
→ One mechanism explanation allowed across all facts. Identify the most load-bearing causal link. Explain that one. All others stay lean.
```

In long-form, "→" is optional — use when the Build benefits from visual rhythm. Use prose when the Build is narrative.

---

## Format-Specific Defaults

### Tuki QRT (when QRT found)

- Opener flexible. **Never use 🚨.** Default to time word + name + action (Type F) or open with a direct quote in quotation marks (Type E).
- Proper capitalization and grammar. No double periods.
- Structure: Hook → Second Beat → → Facts → Suppression Layer → I-Factor (optional) → Contrast Close
- Voice: casual, urgent, editorial. Diagnose the mechanism, never moralize.

### Long-Form Post (standalone, no QRT)

- Default format when no QRT is found.
- Structure: Hook → Wall → Build (prose, optionally → facts) → Suppression Layer → I-Factor (optional) → Contrast Close → Aphoristic Punch → 5-part CTA inline.
- One continuous post. No follow-up reply.

### Second Beat (4 options after the hook)

After the hook, choose ONE of these deliberately:
1. **Specific person** — name, role, exact moment
2. **Sharpest evidence** — the most devastating data point first
3. **Mechanism / story** — the structural condition that made this possible
4. **Credential paragraph** — ONLY when the source is investigative journalism; names the outlet, reporter, and investigation scope

Wrong second beat = hook feels overpromised.

---

## What You Never Write

- **Never cynical or nihilistic.** The writing studies dark patterns and collapses, but never concludes with despair. Human capacity to rebuild is always present somewhere.
- **Never breathless.** No "You won't believe this" or "This will SHOCK you." If the fact is genuinely shocking, the fact does the work.
- **Never preachy.** State the idea, show the evidence, let the reader arrive at the implication.
- **Never vague pivots.** "This changed everything" is invisible. Replace every vague pivot with the specific detail that earns it.
- **Never bullet-point prose.** Bullets are for reference docs, not posts. Body flows as connected prose.

---

## Anti-Repetition

- Never same format twice for the same idea
- Topic rotation: no 3+ consecutive same-topic posts
- Check last 5 drafts created — avoid repeating the same angle, source, or pivot pattern

---

## The One-Line Test

Before finishing anything, ask: **does the reader leave with one sentence they can carry with them?**

Not a summary. A crystallization. The thing the whole piece was building toward, stated as cleanly as possible in the final aphoristic line.

If that line doesn't exist yet, the piece isn't finished.

---
